title: Centrally administered mobility reconsidered: The political dimension of educational stratification in state-socialist Czechoslovakia
abstract: This article investigates the role of political factors in the allocation of educational opportunities in state-socialist Czechoslovakia. The findings indicate that educational reforms introduced after the communist seizure of power in 1948 reduced inequalities in the allocation of schooling at the secondary but not at the tertiary level. This reduction in origin-based inequalities at the secondary level appears to have stemmed not from discrimination on the basis of class background or political behavior, as some have suggested, but from the rapid expansion of the schooling system during the first decades of communist rule. Discrimination on the basis of political criteria is evident, however, during the period following the Soviet invasion of 1968. Among those who reached secondary-school age after 1968, having a father who was a Party member greatly increased the odds of advancing to the secondary educational level but had no effect on the odds of progressing to the tertiary level.
